title: The Master Rules of Volume
subtitle: Volume is the fuel behind every stage change

lessons.title: What Is Trading Volume?
lessons.visual_caption: Volume bars appear at the bottom of every chart. Taller bars mean more shares traded that week.
lessons.content:
Volume is the fuel behind every stage change, so you need to understand it before you study the stages themselves. A move in price means very little until you know how many shares were behind it.

The Definition: Volume is the total number of shares traded — bought and sold — during a given period.

On Your Platform: Volume shows as vertical bars along the bottom of the chart. A tall bar means heavy activity; a short bar means light activity.

Why volume matters:
Any stock can move up on a quiet day when almost nobody is trading. That move means nothing. But when a stock moves up on enormous volume — with millions of shares changing hands — that means institutions are buying aggressively. That is a move you can trust.

→ High volume on an up-day = genuine buying conviction
→ Low volume on an up-day = weak, unconvincing move
→ Volume is the lie detector of price action
→ Never trust a price move without checking volume first

lessons.title: Volume Behaviour Across the Four Stages
lessons.visual_caption: Volume has a distinct pattern in each stage. Learning these four signatures lets you confirm every signal.
lessons.content:
Volume has a distinct signature in each stage. Memorise these four patterns — they are your confirmation tool for every signal you will learn later.

Stage 1 (Basing) — Volume Dries Up:
Pattern: Low, quiet and shrinking.
Meaning: The public has lost interest. There is no aggressive pressure either way. This is actually healthy — the stock is quietly being accumulated.

Stage 2 (Advancing Breakout) — Volume Must Explode:
Pattern: Volume jumps to at least two to three times (2x to 3x) the recent weekly average on the breakout.
Meaning: Institutions are buying with force, pushing price through resistance. Low volume on a breakout means it is suspect.

Stage 3 (Topping) — Volume is Heavy and Chaotic:
Pattern: Volume stays high but price stops advancing and chops sideways.
Meaning: Distribution. Large funds are selling into retail demand. High volume with no price progress is a major warning.

Stage 4 (Declining) — Volume Does Not Matter:
Pattern: Price breaks down on either high or low volume.
Meaning: A lack of buyers lets a stock fall under its own weight. Never wait for high volume to confirm a breakdown.

→ Stage 1: Low quiet volume = healthy base
→ Stage 2: Volume must be 2x-3x on the breakout
→ Stage 3: High chaotic volume with sideways price = danger
→ Stage 4: Breakdown valid on ANY volume level

questions.question: Why is a high-volume spike mandatory during a Stage 2 breakout?
options: It proves retail investors are ignoring the stock
It proves large institutional buyers are entering with force
It signals the 30-week line is about to fall
It confirms a coming Stage 4 downtrend
questions.explanation: Volume is the fuel for a trend. A spike of 2x or more confirms big institutional money is behind the upside breakout. Without it, the breakout is highly likely to fail.

questions.question: True or false: a Stage 4 breakdown must occur on high volume to be a valid sell signal.
options: True — a stock cannot fall into a downtrend on low volume
False — a stock can collapse on low volume simply from a lack of buyers
False — Stage 4 never produces sell signals
questions.explanation: Upside breakouts need buying power (high volume); downside breakdowns do not. With no buyers, price falls on its own. Never wait for high volume before exiting a Stage 4 stock.

questions.question: What does heavy volume combined with sideways price action most likely indicate?
questions.explanation: Heavy volume with no upward price progress is the classic footprint of distribution — large holders selling to excited late buyers. This is a Stage 3 warning, not a buying opportunity.
